summary: Julia Evans reflects on her 2015, highlighting the creation of her first strace zine, giving talks at PyCon, blogging about systems programming and performance, and a successful machine learning project at work. She also candidly discusses dealing with wrist pain from typing all year, which forced her to pivot to drawing comics and be more intentional about what she spends her limited typing energy on. The post balances celebration of creative output with honest acknowledgment of physical limitations, concluding that constraints can sharpen focus on what truly matters.
key_points: Her strace zine was a breakout success, handed out at PyCon and adopted by an OS class, validating the format of teaching systems programming through zines
Blogging serves as both a learning tool and a feedback mechanism — writing things down prevents forgetting, and public discussion surfaces corrections and new ideas
A from-scratch ML project at work converted her lingering skepticism about machine learning into genuine belief that it works when the problem is well-suited
Wrist pain from typing forced a creative pivot to drawing comics and taught her that ignoring physical limitations is unsustainable
Constraints can be focusing — if typing hurts, you stop wasting time on things that don't matter
money_quotes: I was thinking of trying to sell it but it felt like too much work. Instead I spent maybe $300 on printing and gave it away for free, since I have a job.
Blogging is pretty amazing. Writing down what I'm learning helps me not forget.
I don't know how I would have gotten such useful ideas without blogging.
Now I really believe when you have a problem that's well-suited to machine learning, machine learning REALLY WORKS and can make your life awesome.
I've also decided that black box machine learning models that I can't debug are NOT OKAY.
what did not work: pretending that I do not have a body.
If spending 3 hours typing hurts, it makes no sense to work on something in that time that doesn't matter.
key_insight: Physical constraints can sharpen creative focus — when every hour of typing costs something, you stop wasting time on work that doesn't matter.
